ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for prawn crackers; not a clinically coded food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of prawn crackers is approximately 30 g (about a small handful of puffed crackers), providing roughly 153 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — HPB Singapore / IMR Malaysia MyFCD: Prawn Crackers (Keropok Udang / Krupuk Udang)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are drawn from HPB Singapore food composition data for prawn crackers and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for Keropok Udang. Saturated fat estimated from typical palm/vegetable oil deep-frying profile as neither HPB nor MyFCD reports a disaggregated fatty acid breakdown for this item; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat set to -1 accordingly. Cholesterol and water content not reported in either source for this item and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for tapioca-starch-based crackers
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Keropok Udang found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated from the closest analogous food: rice crackers and tapioca-based snack crackers (GI range 70–87 in Atkinson et al. 2021). The predominant carbohydrate source is refined tapioca starch, which is rapidly digested. Deep-frying fat and prawn protein provide only minor moderating effect. Glycemic load per 30 g serving = (75 × 18.15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 13.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (950 mg per 100 g; 285 mg per 30 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit prawn cracker intake; a single 30 g serving delivers approximately 285 mg sodium, representing about 12–19% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 1500–2000 mg, and regular consumption will substantially elevate cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high refined tapioca starch load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn crackers have a high GI of approximately 75 and a glycemic load of ~13.6 per 30 g serving; people with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit consumption to prevent rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes, and should monitor blood glucose if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat from deep-frying (26.2 g/100 g), elevated sodium, and ultra-processed nature
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high total fat (predominantly from deep-frying in vegetable oil), very high sodium, and ultra-processed formulation makes prawn crackers a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or at elevated cardiovascular risk should limit intake to occasional small portions.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Contains crustacean shellfish (prawn) as a primary ingredient
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Prawn crackers contain prawn as a primary ingredient and must be strictly avoided by individuals with a crustacean shellfish allergy; even trace exposure may trigger a severe allergic reaction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Crackers (Keropok Udang in Malay; Krupuk Udang in Indonesian; 虾饼 in Chinese) are a classic deep-fried snack food widely consumed across Southeast Asia, particularly in Malaysia, Indonesia, and Singapore. They are produced by combining minced fresh prawns with tapioca starch, water, salt, and sugar to form a dough, which is then shaped into cylinders, steamed until cooked, sliced into thin rounds, and sun-dried until completely hard and translucent. The dried rounds are deep-fried in hot vegetable oil, causing them to rapidly puff and expand into large, light, exceptionally crispy crackers with a pronounced savory prawn flavor and a delicate, airy texture. Per 100 g, prawn crackers provide approximately 510 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 26.2 g fat, and 60.5 g carbohydrate, with a very high sodium content of 950 mg. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), driven by the refined tapioca starch base. They are high in total fat due to deep-frying and are classified as ultra-processed. Clinically, prawn crackers are a high-concern food for hypertension (very high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high GI and glycemic load), and cardiovascular disease (high fat, high sodium, ultra-processed). They are a major allergen risk for individuals with crustacean shellfish allergy. They are served as a standalone snack or as a crunchy accompaniment to rice and noodle dishes such as Nasi Goreng and Laksa.
common_user_queries: What are prawn crackers made of?
How many calories in prawn crackers?
What is the GI of prawn crackers?
Are prawn crackers high in sodium?
What is the difference between Keropok and Krupuk?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data for prawn crackers and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD; the match is adapted rather than exact as disaggregated fatty acid data (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and water content are not fully reported for this specific item in either database. GI is an analogous estimate from tapioca-based cracker data in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and moisture data for deep-fried prawn crackers are not disaggregated or reported in the HPB Singapore food composition database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD database as of 2024; these fields cannot be populated from approved sources without fabrication. Saturated fat (5.8 g/100 g) is estimated from the typical fatty acid profile of palm/vegetable oil used in commercial deep-frying of starch-based crackers, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for similar fried snack crackers. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace per HPB data; prawn crackers undergo high-heat deep-frying which destroys any residual ascorbic acid. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Keropok Udang was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. This food is a major allergen risk for shellfish-allergic individuals. Multilingual coverage: Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable as this is a Malay/Indonesian dish without established Tamil or Hindi naming conventions; these fields are set to null. Thai and Vietnamese local names are not applicable. Ontology: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are best-available defensible matches; SNOMED CT has no specific code for prawn crackers.
